coredata.dc:title: Architecting ultra-bright silanized carbon dots by alleviating the spin-orbit coupling effect: a specific fluorescent nanoprobe to label dead cells
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jianying
Liu, Wei-Ren
Li, Yanjuan
Zou, Xikun
Li, Wei
Liang, Jiarong
Zhang, Haoran
Liu, Yingliang
Zhang, Xuejie
Hu, Chaofan
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: Developing high-performance carbon dots (CDs) with high photoluminescence quantum yield, excellent stability, and good biocompatibility as well as explore the related luminescence mechanism is still an urgent problem to be solved at present. Herein, we develop a new class of green-emitting silanized carbon dots (Si-CDs) featuring average size of ~ 2.8 nm, ultrahigh photoluminescence quantum yield of ~ 93%, robust photostability, and low cytotoxicity by high-temperature dehalogenation/polymerization process. Our experiments verify the dehalogenation process can alleviate the spin–orbit coupling (SOC) effect of heavy-atom-rich organic fluorophores with severe intersystem crossing through high-temperature hydrothermal reaction, meanwhile, the possibility of transition from the excited state (S1) to triplet state (T1) in the Si-CDs is significantly reduced, thus enhancing the fluorescence intensity and prolong fluorescent lifetime of Si-CDs. Besides, multiple fluorescent emitters can be integrated into one nanodot to effectively enhance the emission intensity by cross-linking polymerization. Owing to good optical properties of the obtained Si-CDs, when used as fluorescent nanoprobes, Si-CDs can rapidly and universally label dead bacteria, fungi, and mammalian cells, which can meet the needs of green fluorescent nanoprobes for specific imaging of dead cells, so as to fast discriminate live/dead cells. We believe that Si-CDs with excellent optical performance, ultrasmall particle size, and low cytotoxicity can be applied to the fields of biological imaging, optical devices, and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silanized carbon dots
High photoluminescence quantum yield
Spin-orbit coupling
Fluorescent nanoprobes
Label dead cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Staining performance of Si-CDs to discriminate Live/Dead cells
Photostability comparison of Si-CDs and PI in dead S. aureus cells
Morphological and structural characterizations of Si-CDs
Optical performance and PL mechanisms of Si-CDs
Application of a specific fluorescent probe to label dead cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundations of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 21671070 ), the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI, the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2021KJ122), and the Guangdong Provincial Science & Technology Project (No. 2020A1414010049).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis strategy of Si-CDs.

(a) Lateral size distributions, (b) TEM images and (c) HRTEM images of Si-CDs. (d) FTIR spectra of Si-CDs, APTMs and EB. (e) Survey XPS spectrum of Si-CDs. (f) Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) of Si-CDs and Si-CDs B-Htt (Si-CDs before hydrothermal treatment). And the high resolution XPS peaks of (d) Si2p, (e) O1s, (f) N1s, and (g) C1s of Si-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption of Si-CDs solution and EB solution after hydrothermal. (b) Fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of Si-CDs solution and EB solution after hydrothermal. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of Si-CDs excited at different wavelengths from 360 to 480 nm with an increment of 20 nm. EPR results of singlet oxygen generated by (d) Si-CDs and (f) EB after 808 nm irradiation (3.99 mW/cm2, 10 min). (g) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of Si-CDs solution; (h) Energy level diagram of Si-CDs, indicating fluorescence enhancement by breaking spin–orbit coupling (SOC) of organic fluorophore to suppress intersystem crossing (ISC) transition (i) Mechanism diagram of Si-CDs by enhancing the brightness at the single particle level through integrating multi-emitters within one nanodot.

(a) Confocal fluorescence images of live and dead S. aureus cells treated with various concentration of Si-CDs, Confocal fluorescence images of live and dead S. aureus cells treated with various time of Si-CDs (20 μg/mL). (b) Confocal images of different biological live and dead cells after treatment with Si-CDs (20 μg/mL, 5 min).

(a) Colocalization fluorescence images of Si-CDs (20 μg/mL) and PI (20 μg/mL) in different types of dead cells. (b) Photostability evaluation results of Si-CDs (20 μg/mL), and PI (20 μg/mL) in S. aureus cells via confocal fluorescence imaging.
Relative and absolute PLQYs of Si-CDs, D-Si-CDs, and A-Si-CDs measured at their maximum excitation wavelengths of 490 nm 490 nm and 480 nm, respectively.

head.ce:title: Architecting ultra-bright silanized carbon dots by alleviating the spin-orbit coupling effect: a specific fluorescent nanoprobe to label dead cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Maoming Branch, Guangdong Laboratory for Lingnan Modern Agriculture, Guangdong Maoming 525100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Laboratory for Lingnan Modern Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Maoming Branch, Guangdong Laboratory for Lingnan Modern Agriculture, Guangdong Maoming 525100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan City 32023, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan City 32023, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Through high temperature dehalogenation/polymerization process, a new class of green-emitting silanized carbon dots (Si-CDs) featuring ultrahigh photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is developed. The mechanism of improving the PLQY of Si-CDs mainly includes two steps. Firstly, the dehalogenation process can alleviate the spin–orbit coupling (SOC) effect of heavy-atom-rich organic fluorophores with severe intersystem crossing through high temperature hydrothermal reaction, meanwhile the possibility of transition from excited state (S1) to triplet state (T1) in the Si-CDs is significantly reduced, thus enhancing the fluorescence intensity and prolong fluorescence lifetime of Si-CDs. Besides, multiple fluorescent emitters can be integrated into one nano dot to effectively enhance the emission intensity in a single nano dot level by cross-linking polymerization. Moreover, Si-CDs can be used as fluorescent nanoprobes, which can rapidly and universally label dead cells, thus fast discriminate live/dead cells.

Developing high-performance carbon dots (CDs) with high photoluminescence quantum yield, excellent stability, and good biocompatibility as well as explore the related luminescence mechanism is still an urgent problem to be solved at present. Herein, we develop a new class of green-emitting silanized carbon dots (Si-CDs) featuring average size of ~ 2.8 nm, ultrahigh photoluminescence quantum yield of ~ 93%, robust photostability, and low cytotoxicity by high-temperature dehalogenation/polymerization process. Our experiments verify the dehalogenation process can alleviate the spin–orbit coupling (SOC) effect of heavy-atom-rich organic fluorophores with severe intersystem crossing through high-temperature hydrothermal reaction, meanwhile, the possibility of transition from the excited state (S1) to triplet state (T1) in the Si-CDs is significantly reduced, thus enhancing the fluorescence intensity and prolong fluorescent lifetime of Si-CDs. Besides, multiple fluorescent emitters can be integrated into one nanodot to effectively enhance the emission intensity by cross-linking polymerization. Owing to good optical properties of the obtained Si-CDs, when used as fluorescent nanoprobes, Si-CDs can rapidly and universally label dead bacteria, fungi, and mammalian cells, which can meet the needs of green fluorescent nanoprobes for specific imaging of dead cells, so as to fast discriminate live/dead cells. We believe that Si-CDs with excellent optical performance, ultrasmall particle size, and low cytotoxicity can be applied to the fields of biological imaging, optical devices, and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:para: In the research of applications in biomedical field, the detection of cell viability is an indispensable index to identify the efficacy of drugs, and cell viability often depends on the identification of living cells and dead cells in the research of related fields [1,2]. However, an efficient live/dead cell identification is still lacking. At present, several typical methods have been developed, such as atomic force microscopy, electron microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, field-of-view (FOV) diffraction microscopy, and synchrotron radiation infrared microscopy spectroscopy to identify live/dead microorganisms or mammalian cells [3–8]. Compared with the above methods, fluorescence labeling technology has the advantages of simple operation, rapid reaction, and high sensitivity. Generally speaking, fluorescence labeling reagent used to distinguish between living cells and dead cells could be roughly divided into two categories [9], colorimetric agent (3- (4, 5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl) −2, 5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), cell counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) kit,) and fluorescent probe (Propidium iodide (PI), SYTOX green nucleic acid stain (abbreviated as SYTOX), trypan blue, fluorescein diacetate (FDA), and calcein acetoxymethyl (Calcein AM), etc.). Colorimetric agent is a commonly used reagent in the research of cytotoxicity and cell proliferation, but they are not suitable for long-term cell viability monitoring or need a complicated operation flow [10,11]. On the contrary, fluorescent probe has higher sensitivity and in situ imaging ability for cells and tissues, so it is a more favorable one. In the current commercial fluorescent probes, PI, SYTOX and trypan blue which can be used to distinguish live cells from dead cells due to they can permeate into the cell membrane of dead cells. Fluorescein diacetate, and Calcein AM can selectively stain living cells, which is due to the difference between the metabolic capabilities of the dead and live cells, thus they can permeate cell membrane and accumulate in living cells as fluorescein [12–16]. However, these fluorescent probes have the disadvantages of highly toxic and poor photo-stability, which can’t be ignored. Nanoscale carbon dots (CDs) could fill this gap because of their good biocompatibility and excellent optical properties, which they can solve the toxic effects of fluorescent pigments on cells and the interference of cell self-fluorescence [17–19]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new fluorescent carbon dots with excellent optical properties and high biocompatibility to realize rapid and sensitive differentiation of living cells and dead cells.

Generally speaking, carbon dots refer to zero dimensional nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm, due to their different synthesis strategies, induce covalent crosslinking of precursors and generate abundant surface groups, which determine the physical and chemical properties, such as particle size, catalytic performance, photo-tunability, and easy surface modification capability, etc. [20–25] . Moreover, in recent years, compare to organic fluorescent dye and other fluorescent nanomaterials such as semiconductor fluorescent quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, and noble metal nanoclusters [26–28], carbon dots have been widely used in fluorescent probes, light-emitting devices, biosensors, biological imaging, and anti-counterfeiting, due to their outstanding advantages such as excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, simple synthesis process, and low-cost sources [29–35]. More notably, silanized carbon dots are based on indirect band gap semiconductors, which not only have the same good photoluminescence characteristics as toxic metal-based quantum dots, but also have the additional benefits of better biocompatibility and earth richness [36,37]. According to the different properties and synthesis routes of precursors and silanes, the properties and luminescence mechanism of prepared silanized carbon dots are often different, so the selection of precursors and the control of synthesis routes are also extremely important. To date, high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) is of great significance for practical applications, but the high-efficiency luminous source of carbon dots is still blurred. Thus, it is very necessary to explore the luminescence mechanism of high quantum yield silanized carbon dots.

Intersystem crossing (ISC) plays an important role in determining the optical properties of luminescent materials, especially those with heavy atoms. ISC plays a leading role in enhancing the luminescence of phosphorescent materials, but it will seriously attenuate the fluorescence intensity [38,39]. ISC from singlet to triplet induced by strong state of charge will seriously reduce the number of singlet excited states, resulting in a sharp decline in fluorescence intensity and lifetime [40,41]. Therefore, the destruction of spin–orbit coupling (SOC) can weaken the severe ISC and enhance the fluorescence intensity of organic fluorophores [42,43]. In addition, integrating multiple emitters into a fluorescent nanodot can effectively improve the emission intensity of a single nanoparticle. Based on these theories, the (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMs) as a mediated substrate, Erythrosin B (EB) as a representative heavy-atoms-rich organic fluorophore to support strong SOC were selected in this work. Through a high temperature hydrothermal reaction, we synthesized ultra-small and bright green emitting silanized carbon dots (Si-CDs) with photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of exceeding 90%, excellent photo-stability, low cytotoxicity, and small size below 3 nm. Proved by experiments, the ultra-bright luminescence of silanized carbon dots attribute to the heavy atom loss caused by hydrothermal reaction, which can alleviate SOC to enhance fluorescence, as well as polymerization and cross-linking of multiple emission centers can further enhance fluorescence emission intensity. Moreover, Si-CDs to be served as a specific fluorescent nanoprobe to identify dead cells, which realize rapid, efficient and universal differentiation of living/dead bacteria, fungi and mammalian cells. In addition, because of the advantages of simple synthesis, low cost, low toxicity and good optical properties, we hope that Si-CDs can be designed as fluorescent nanoprobes with low cost and high sensitivity.

1 mg of EB was dissolved in 4 mL of water, injected nitrogen and stir for 10 min to remove oxygen. After the addition of 1 mL APTMs, nitrogen is also pumped in and stirred for 10 min. And then the mixture was transferred into a 10 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, after keeping at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 h, the reaction was cooled down to room temperature. And then the resultant solution was dialyzed (500 Da) 24 h to remove the residual reagents. The final sample was collected through freeze drying and kept at 4 °C for use. AEEA silanized carbon dots and DAMO silanized carbon dots were prepared at a temperature of 160 °C for 6 h by a similar method as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Staining performance of Si-CDs to discriminate Live/Dead cells
ce:section.ce:para: Already prepared live and dead S. aureus cells were stained with different concentrations of Si-CDs solution (10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL or 100 μg/mL) and stained for different time (1 min, 5 min, 15 min or 30 min) with 20 μg/mL of Si-CDs solution. In addition, escherichia coli cells, Italian penicillium cells, AH109 yeast cells or HepG2 cells were stained with 20 μg/mL of Si-CDs solution for 5 min. Then washed with normal saline and imaged under a confocal microscope. The Si-CDs were excited at 480 nm and the emission was collected in the range of 495–545 nm.

As for compared the staining performance of Si-CDs and PI, dead S. aureus cells, E. coli cells, Italian penicillium cells, and HepG2 cells were co-stained with Si-CDs solution (20 μg/mL) and PI (20 μg/mL) for 5 min, then washed with normal saline and imaged under a confocal microscope. The Si-CDs were excited at 480 nm and the images were collected in the emission range of 495–545 nm. PI was excited at 555 nm and the images were collected in the emission range of 600–650 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability comparison of Si-CDs and PI in dead S. aureus cells
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of photostability of Si-CDs and PI in dead S. aureus cells was also conducted. Dead S. aureus were separately stained with Si-CDs solution (20 μg/mL) and PI (20 μg/mL) for 5 min followed by washing with normal saline. Then the as-prepared dead S. aureus cells were respectively exposure under the 480 nm and 555 nm laser for different times (0s, 5s, 30s, 1 min, 5 min, 10 min, 15 min or 30 min), and the corresponding images were respectively obtained in the emission range of 600–650 nm by a confocal microscope.

Through a hydrolytic polymerization/dehalogenation hydrothermal reaction of (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) and Erythrosin B (EB) in water, we synthesized ultra-small, bright green-emitting water-soluble silanized carbon dots (Si-CDs). As to obtain high quantum yield of silanized carbon dots, the optimal synthesis route was explored by optimizing synthesis condition such as temperature, time and raw material proportion. As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescent Si-CDs were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction between EB and APTMs at 180 °C for 6 h. In order to explore the universality of luminescent properties of silanized carbon dots, we also designed and synthesized two other silanized carbon dots by using the other two silane molecules (DAMO and AEEA) respectively. DAMO and AEEA have similar structures to APTMS, but are different from APTMS in the number of amine groups in each silane molecule [44]. In addition, the same optimization method as Si-CDs was used to synthesize DAMO silanized carbon dots (represented as D-Si-CDs) with N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO) and EB at 160 °C for 6 h (Fig. S1a). AEEA silanized carbon dots (represented as A-Si-CDs) were prepared by the hydrothermal treatment of the aqueous mixture containing EB and 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino) ethylamino] propyl-trimethoxysilane (AEEA) at 160 °C for 6 h (Fig. S1b). Three types of silanized carbon dots keep clear aqueous solution at room temperature for a long time, and all show super green emission under 365 nm UV light irradiation, indicating they had consistent chemical changes during the synthesis process. In the schematic illustration of synthesis strategy (Fig. 1), the heavy-atom-rich EB molecule as organic fluorophore and the silane molecule as mediating substance to be dispersed in the water molecule and adding nitrogen to get rid of oxygen, under the condition of high temperature and high pressure, the silane molecule and the EB molecule preliminarily undergo dehydration and dehalogenation reaction respectively. In addition, a series of polymerization cross-linking reactions such as acylation reaction between amino groups of silane molecules and carbonyl groups of EB molecules further form silanized carbon dots. The loss of heavy atoms (Iodine) and the integration of nano-dot emission centers in the reaction make the silanized carbon dots have strong green-emitting fluorescence. The series course of reactions is confirmed in the following discussion. Furthermore, the non-fluorescent solution obtained after thorough dialysis of silanized carbon dots shows that the dehalogenated organic fluorophores are covalently crosslinked with silane molecules to form new nano-polymers. Through comparison and comprehensive consideration, the Si-CDs obtained from APTMs (represented as Si-CDs) were used as the representative in the following characterization and a fluorescent nanoprobe in discrimination of live/dead cells in the further research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterizations of Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of these samples was observed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The characterization of the as-prepared fluorescent silanized carbon dots by TEM shows Si-CDs are uniform nanodots with a mean diameter of ~ 2.8 nm (Fig. 2 a), which is even smaller than that of fluorescent proteins (~ 4 nm), and resulting Si-CDs also have spherical morphology with good monodispersity (Fig. 2b). Moreover, the other two silanized carbon dots also have spherical morphology with good monodispersity (Fig. S2a, d). However, the average particle size of D-Si-CDs is about 8.5 nm (Fig. S2c), the average particle size of A-Si-CDs is about 9.3 nm (Fig. S2f), which are greater than Si-CDs. We think that the large size of D-Si-CDs and A-Si-CDs is attributed to their higher polymerization degree, because they have more groups such as amino groups for modification. In the HRTEM images, (Fig. 2c for Si-CDs, Fig. S2b for D-Si-CDs, and Fig. S2e for A-Si-CDs), the same lattice fringes spacing value of each of three Si-CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponds to the (100) facet of graphite, indicating that three Si-CDs contain graphite-like structures. The average height of Si-CDs is about 2.3 nm, manifesting that they are primarily consisted of a few layers of graphite-like sheets, and the spherical morphology is further proved (Fig. S3) [22]. Small-sized Si-CDs are more conducive to subsequent use, especially in the biomedical field. Besides, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum indicates a distinct peak at around 21.5°, signifying the amorphous nature and reveals the amorphous silica nature of these Si-CDs (Fig. S4).

To confirm the presence of chemical composition and various functional groups in Si-CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out. The FTIR spectra of Si-CDs (Fig. 2d) exhibit some distinct absorption peaks ranging from 1000 to 3500 cm−1, and show that the formed Si-CDs contain C-H, C-O, C-N, O-H, N-H, and Si-O-Si groups. Shown as Fig. S5, the cases for D-Si-CDs and A-Si-CDs are also similar to Si-CDs, suggesting similar surface functional groups and chemical bonds of three types of Si-CDs. Furthermore, to explore the sources of functional groups, chemical bonds of Si-CDs, the FTIR spectra and XPS spectra were further analyzed. The stretching vibration of Si-O-Si occurs at 1032 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2d), indicating that silica-like structures were formed in Si-CDs via the partial crosslinking between the hydrolyzed APTMs molecules during the hydrothermal reaction, which can also be verified by Si2p signal (Fig. 2g), and O1s signal (Si-O: 531.2 eV, Fig. 2h) in the XPS spectra [45]. The strong signals at 1575 and 3400 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibration and the stretching vibration of the N–H bond (Fig. 2d), respectively, it is consistent with N–H bone (399.1 eV) were observed in N1s signal (Fig. 2i) of XPS spectrum. These results show that the reaction between the carbonyl group of EB and the amine group of APTMs to form amide bond during the hydrothermal treatment, which may also contribute to the formation of the final Si-CDs. The narrow feature at 3500 cm−1 should be attributed to the O-H stretching. The O-H groups come from fracture of carbon-oxygen single bond or neutralization reaction of EB molecule, which indicates that a series of interactions have taken place between silane molecules and EB. Moreover, the stretching vibration of C-O occurs at 1330 cm−1 and C-N occurs at 1124 cm−1, the stretching vibration of 2936 cm−1 and bending vibration of 1283 cm−1 is attributed to the C–H can be observed in FTIR spectra (Fig. 2d), what may come from the formed amide groups or the adsorbed EB molecules and silane molecule of Si-CDs, which closely related to N1s signal (Fig. 2i) and C1s signal (Fig. 2j) of XPS data. It is worth noting that, compare with the raw material, the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2d) of Si-CDs resembles that of the reactant (APTMs molecules), indicating that Si-CDs are coated in the network mediation of silane.

In Fig. 2e, four elements can be observed in the XPS spectrum of Si-CDs including C (59.42%), O (20.29%), N (8.84%), and Si (11.45%). I (from EB molecules) could not be detected, it has also been confirmed in electron energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 2f). These analyses show that the hydrothermal dehalogenation process took place in the silicon alkylation nano dots, and I (from EB molecule) of Si-CDs is completely lost after dialysis, indicating the loss of halide atoms after the hydrothermal reaction (due to the instability of C–I bonds at high temperatures [46]) and dialysis.

From these results, we guessed the process of the synthesis of Si-CDs as following: the hydrolyzed silane molecules were partially crosslinked and the EB molecules lost their halide atoms by hydrothermal reaction. Meanwhile, the EB molecules were conjugated with the silane molecules via the reaction between the carbonyl and the amine groups at high temperatures. In short, the intramolecular dehydrated silane molecules crosslink with dehalogenated EB to form a silanized carbon core with a silica-like network structure and green-emitting fluorescent amorphous polymer shell through the high temperature polymerization process. Such a series of structural reorganizations contribute to the final formation of Si-CDs with a silica-like structure, and we speculate that the Si-CDs consists of a crosslinked amorphous silanized carbon core with covalently attached dehalogenation fluorescent dye molecular fragments. In addition, these processes preliminarily verified our hypothesis that the strong fluorescence emission of fluorescent Si-CDs is due to the loss of heavy atoms, which relieves the SOC effect. On the other hand, the fluorescent Si-CDs may be composed of organic silicon cores with covalently linked multiple emission centers, which makes it possible to integrate multiple emitters in a single nano dot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performance and PL mechanisms of Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the fluorescent properties of silanized carbon dots, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained. The UV–vis spectra of the three types of silanized carbon dots (Fig. 3 a of Si-CDs, Fig. S6a of D-Si-CDs, and Fig. S6d of A-Si-CDs) show that they all possess good optical properties with a clear absorption peak. In addition, the UV–vis spectra show that the Si-CDs and D-Si-CDs show a shoulder absorption peak at 240 nm which could attribute to the π → π* transition and three silanized carbon dots exhibit a strong absorption peak at 490 nm which could attribute to the n → π* transition of trapping surface states related to C=O. The existence of this peak might be due to the trapping of exited-state energy by the surface state, which leads to a strong fluorescence emission. Accordingly, the fluorescence spectra show the maximum excitation peak of Si-CDs (Fig. 3b), D-Si-CDs (Fig. S6b), and A-Si-CDs (Fig. S6e) located at 490 nm, 490 nm, 480 nm, respectively, which is consistent with the relevant excitonic absorption band, suggesting that the nonradiative transition has hardly occurred in the Si-CDs. Notably, Si-CDs have a narrow excitation-independent fluorescence emission peak at 516 nm (FWHM ≈ 38 nm) (Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c) due to the highly ordered graphitic structure [47], which similar to the other two Si-CDs (Fig. S6b, c, e, f), indicates three silanized carbon dots have similar luminescent properties, because the luminescence of carbon dots comes from EB luminescent fluorophores. More surprisingly, the Si-CDs retain the long-term photostability (retain about 80% emission intensity after four days) under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. S7), which attribute to the inherent optical properties of silicon-based nanomaterials [48], and their silica-like structure can protect fluorescence from photobleaching of oxygen molecules [45]. Furthermore, the Si-CDs fluorescence results correspond to the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates of (0.23, 0.68) (Fig. S8), which is close to the spectral locus, indicating the saturated green emission of Si-CDs, which can be used as a novel green phosphor for the potential application of light-emitting diodes (LEDs).

The relative PLQYs of the three types of silanized carbon dots were calculated with rhodamine 6G (the quantum yield of dissolving in ethanol is 95%) as the reference. Using the reported methods, we could obtain relative quantum yield values of Si-CDs, D-Si-CDs, and A-Si-CDs are PLQY ~ 93.8%, PLQY ~ 81.7%, and PLQY ~ 73.6%, respectively. Meanwhile, we have also collected the absolute QY measurement using an absolute quantum yield measurement system with integrating sphere. As shown in Fig. S9, the absolute PLQY values of Si-CDs (Fig. S9a, b), D-Si-CDs (Fig. S9c, d), and A-Si-CDs (Fig. S9e, f) were measured to be 93.23%, 86.29%, and 77.23%, respectively, which agree well with the above relative PLQY values (Table 1 ). The much higher PLQY of Si-CDs is also confirmed by the photograph pictured under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. 1). Also shown in Fig. S10 is the photograph of the Si-CDs aqueous solution and hand-written letters “S”, “C”, and “U” on the pieces of filter paper using Si-CDs, D-Si-CDs, and A-Si-CDs as fluorescent inks (irradiated under 365 nm UV light), respectively. Be consistent with the PLQYs, the fluorescence emission intensity of the letter “S” written by APTMs Si-CDs solution is much higher than that of the other two letters. By reason of these Si-CDs solutions are clear and colorless at low concentrations (Fig. S10), they can suitably be used for anticounterfeiting application.

To explore the luminescence mechanism of silanized carbon dots, we chose Si-CDs as representatives. Therefore, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the reactant APTMs and EB aqueous solution before and after the hydrothermal treatment were obtained (Fig. S11a–d). As shown in Fig. S11a, there is hardly observed the difference between APTMs before and after hydrothermal, but the yellow emission of the EB aqueous solution before hydrothermal treatment is far weaker than which after hydrothermal treatment, verifying the loss of heavy atoms at high temperature to improve the emission intensity of Si-CDs. Notably, green emitting fluorescence is weaker than Si-CDs (PLQY ~ 93.23%) was observed after hydrothermal treatment of EB aqueous solution (PLQY ~ 43.64%), which indicates that both EB and silane molecules covalent cross-linking are essential for preparing Si-CDs. The EB molecules has a conjugated structure and chemically rigid structure, which can enhance fluorescence emission, but its high content of halide atoms decreased its fluorescence intensity due to the “heavy atom effect” [49], because of replacing an atom with a higher atomic number results in an increased probability of triplet formation, and thereby inducing photoanaphysis and reducing the PL efficiency and lifetime of fluorescence processes [45]. The EB molecule has ultra-weak fluorescence emission when it is rich in heavy atoms, which indicates that the SOC effect of heavy atoms open the nonradiative ISC channel to weakens or quenches the fluorescence emission. ISC has become the main decay pathway, and heavy atoms are effective triplet promoters [50]. After the hydrothermal treatment of EB and silane molecules, the cross-linking polymerization and dehalogenation process can alleviate the spin–orbit coupling (SOC) effect of heavy atom organic fluorophores, thus weaken the severe ISC and enhance the fluorescence intensity of organic fluorophores [51]. Meanwhile, the possibility of transition from excited state (S1) to triplet state (T1) in the Si-CDs is significantly reduced, thus enhancing the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence lifetime of fluorophores with severe intersystem crossing (Fig. 4 h). Meanwhile, the XPS (Fig. 2e) and EDS result (Fig. 2f) indicates the occurrence of the dehalogenation reaction during the hydrothermal treatment, which could significantly reduce the transition possibility of the excited state (S1) to the triple state (T1) in Si-CDs, leading to the stronger fluorescence emission. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) (Fig. 3d, f) and time-resolved fluorescent decay curve (Fig. 3g) of Si-CDs confirm this point. The singlet oxygen proton yield can reflect the ability of luminescent materials to produce triplets through intersystem crossing, the high yield of singlet oxygen can reflect the superiority of transition from singlet state to triplet state[52] For pre-unreacted precursor (Without dehalogenation), the high yield intense singlet oxygen (1O2) signal was observed after irradiation (Fig. 3d), while under the same conditions, Si-CDs solution (dehalogenation) hardly generate singlet oxygen signal (Fig. 3f). The fluorescence lifetime of Si-CDs, D-Si-CDs, and A-Si-CDs was calculated to be 4.48 ns (Fig. 3g), 4.55 ns (Fig. S12b) and 4.32 ns (Fig. S12c), respectively, which was much longer than EB (the fluorescence lifetime of EB solution is almost undetectable) (Fig. S12a), further proving the heavy atom effect loss to decrease the probability of forming a triplet state, which intuitively reflects the alleviation of spin–orbit coupling caused by heavy atomic effect. [45] Otherwise, the monoexponential fluorescence lifetime decay curve proves that there is only one luminous center in Si-CDs, indicates there is no energy transfer between Si-CDs and EB to influence on fluorescence emission intensity of Si-CDs [53]. Moreover, to further explore the influence of spin-orbit coupling on luminescence of Si-CDs, we further measured the luminescence of EB molecules and Si-CDs dispersed in glycerol/water mixture to controlled the solvent viscosity and dissolved oxygen content gradient [43]. In the case of pure EB solution, we found that the phosphorescence from the triplet radiative decay starts emerging which proof by the phosphorescence lifetime signal (Fig. S13). However, the phosphorescence lifetimes of EB solution remains unchanged with the decrease of solvent viscosity and the increase of dissolved oxygen, indicating that the triple radiation phosphorescence is insensitive to dissolved oxygen. On the contrary, the phosphorescence lifetime signal of Si-CDs cannot be detected, even when the influence of dissolved oxygen is completely eliminated. Therefore, we believe that the spin–orbit coupling effect of Si-CDs is greatly suppressed, thus enhancing the singlet-states fluorescence of Si-CDs. What is more worth exploring is that, as shown in Fig. S14, the spin-orbit coupling constant obtained by energy band between split peaks of ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) of Si-CDs (1.30 eV, Fig. S14a) is smaller than that of EB (1.55 eV, Fig. S14b), indicating the loss of heavy atoms can alleviate the spin–orbit coupling of Si-CDs. And the UPS of Si-CDs exhibits that a cut-off energy (Ef) is 15.27 eV, and the HOMO was obtained is −4.10 eV. Moreover, combined with the energy gap (2.41 eV) was obtained from ultraviolet absorption spectrum, so the LUMO is −1.69 eV. In the same way, a cut-off energy (Ef) of EB is 16.49 eV, the HOMO was obtained is −3.77 eV. combined with the energy gap (2.25 eV), the LUMO level is −1.52 eV. The lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of Si-CDs compared with that of EB, so it is thermodynamically favoring the occurrence of the internal electron transfer from EB to Si-CDs [54].

Most notably, when EB as a fluorophore is not dispersed in APTMs under the same hydrothermal conditions, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the two samples are also highly consistent, but the absorption peak of Si-CDs is obviously stronger (Fig. 3a). Moreover, the optimal excitation and emission peaks of EB are consistent with those of Si-CDs, but the emission intensity of EB solution is much weaker than that of Si-CDs (Fig. 3b). The above results prove once again that the interaction between EB and APTMs can effectively form strong fluorescent carbon dots. The hydrothermal dehalogenation of EB alone plays a part in fluorescence enhancement, while the polymerization and crosslinking of silane molecules and EB molecules make multiple fluorescence emission centers integrated into a single nanodot, thus enhancing fluorescence (Fig. 3i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of a specific fluorescent probe to label dead cells
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent photostability (Fig. S7) and pH stability (Fig. S15) of Si-CDs prompt us to study their behavior in cells. In the preliminary experiment, we designed to use Si-CDs to image living cells and dead cells (obtained by treatment with 1% benzalkonium bromide for 2 h or ethanol for 0.5 h). When excited at 480 nm, we found that Si-CDs can label dead cells in a short time, but cannot label living cells in a confocal microscope. Thus, Si-CDs can be used to differentiation of live/dead cells.

To evaluate the staining performance of Si-CDs to distinguish dead/live cells, the gram-positive bacteria staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) cells were chosen as a representative. To begin with, the different treated concentrations of Si-CDs (Fig. S16) for S. aureus cells was performed. When excited at 480 nm, it was found that live cells were hardly stained by all the tested concentrations of Si-CDs, while the strong fluorescence signals of dead cells (obtained by treatment with 1% benzalkonium bromide for 2 h) even at concentration as low as 10 μg/mL of Si-CDs were observed in a confocal microscope (Fig. 4a). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity changes of Si-CDs solution with different concentrations are shown in the Fig. 4a. Also, the effect of the staining time on the live/dead cell discrimination performance of Si-CDs was evaluated. The strong fluorescence signal of dead cells was observed after incubation with Si-CDs for all staining times, even at 1 min. In particular, the fluorescence intensity of the dead cells was stained for 1 min was high as that at 5 min, 15 min or 30 min. However, the fluorescence signals of live S. aureus cells were hardly observed in a confocal microscope (Fig. 4a), indicating that the intact cell surfaces of the live S. aureus cells were impermeable to the nanodots. All in all, low concentrations of Si-CDs solution can also selectively stain dead S. aureus cells, not live S. aureus cells, and Si-CDs can quickly penetrate dead cells for staining, indicating the Si-CDs can used as a good fluorescent probe to distinguish living and dead cells.

To prove the universality and imaging performance of Si-CDs for different types of dead cells staining, the bacteria (S. aureus, E. coli) cells, fungi (AH109 yeast, penicillium italicum) cells and mammalian cells (HepG2 cells) were taken as representatives of different types cell. As shown in Fig. 4b, the strong fluorescent signals were observed in the dead cells which stained with 20 μg/mL of Si-CDs for 5 min, but the live cells are difficult to stain with Si-CDs, even if the dying time is prolonged to 1 h. Moreover, the results what we obtained indicating that Si-CDs can be a universal probe to differentiate live cells from dead cells regardless of the cell types. We speculated that the reason for this result is that the pathway of Si-CDs uptake by live/dead cells is different. The mechanism of live/dead cell staining can be explained by the significant difference between the two kinds cell uptake pathways. The intact cell membrane of live cells hinders the penetration of Si-CDs, and live cells generally uptake Si-CDs slowly by endocytosis. However, compared to the slow internalization of Si-CDs by live cells, Si-CDs can quickly penetrate dead cells with damaged cell membranes (Fig. S17) by passive diffusion. Only dead cells can observe the fluorescence signal of Si-CDs, thus the effective distinction between live cells and dead cells is realized.

To further prove the specificity of Si-CDs as fluorescent nanoprobe in distinguishing live and dead cells, we chose fluorescent dyes PI as comparison. PI can only penetrate cell-damaged membranes and stain the cell nucleus, therefore it is widely used as a commercial dead cell fluorescent probe and can be used to distinguish dead cells from live cells. The experimental results show that the staining performance of Si-CDs on dead cells is similar to PI. Due to validate the selective dead cell imaging ability of Si-CDs, the PI was chosen as a reference. We selected different types of cells to co-stain with PI and Si-CDs. In the confocal images, strong green fluorescence for Si-CDs can be observed, and strong red fluorescence for PI can be observed in each type of cells (Fig. 5 a). Moreover, strong yellow signals were observed in the merged confocal image (Fig. 5a), which manifests the excellent colocalization of Si-CDs and PI, and also fully prove that Si-CDs can be used to be a specific fluorescent probe to distinguish dead cells.

To further verify the favorable property of photobleaching resistance of Si-CDs, we compared the photostability of Si-CDs with PI in S. aureus. In confocal images, compared with performance of PI whose fluorescence markedly decreased within 15 min and completely quenched within 30 min of 535 nm laser irradiation, Si-CDs still exhibited strong fluorescence after 30 min of 480 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 5b). The above results show that the Si-CDs have the potential to be used for long-term stable monitoring of the dead cells. Furthermore, the biotoxicity of Si-CDs using HepG2 cells as representative was evaluated, the parallel tests for PI were also carried out for comparison. As shown in Fig. S18, PI exhibited significantly higher cytotoxicity to HepG2 cells than Si-CDs. The cell survival rate could be maintained even 97.23% when the concentration of Si-CDs was 500 μg/mL, but the cell survival rate maintained only 61.99% when the concentration of PI was 200 μg/mL. In a word, the excellent optical properties of silicon quantum dots meet the requirements of using them as fluorescent probes for labeling dead cells, including good photostability, low cytotoxicity, and extremely high fluorescence quantum yield.

In summary, three kinds of silanized carbon dots with good stability and high fluorescence quantum yield were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process. Results show that the three kinds of silanized carbon dots show similar structure and luminescence properties. However, compared with A-Si-CDs and D-Si-CDs, Si-CDs has the properties of smaller particle size of ~ 2.8 nm, higher fluorescence quantum yield of ~ 93.23%. As for the luminescence mechanism of silanized carbon dots, through a silane-mediated hydrolytic condensation/dehalogenation process, silane molecules are closely cross-linked with fluorescein, so that multiple emitters can be integrated into a single fluorescent nanodot, and ISC induced by SOC can be effectively suppressed, thus improving the fluorescence quantum yield. The cross-linking and recombination between silane molecules and fluorescent pigment molecules makes the surface groups of silanized carbon dots more abundant, and the surface defects of silicon dots act as the capture centers of excitons, thus causing surface state related fluorescence. Moreover, the results show that due to Si-CDs could interact strongly with the dead cells with damaged cell membrane and aggregation in cell nucleus as well as Si-CDs could not enter the complete cell membrane of living cells in a short time, the prepared Si-CDs with low toxicity and good optical properties have been selected as the representative of application as specific fluorescent probes to differentiation of live/dead cells. We believe that the carbon dots with ultra-high fluorescence quantum yield, low toxicity and good stability have the potential to be used as biological and chemical fluorescent probes, and can be used as ideal targets for anti-counterfeiting and led green phosphors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 21671070), the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI, the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2021KJ122), and the Guangdong Provincial Science & Technology Project (No. 2020A1414010049).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.131168.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
